0.title: mip alias - command shortcuts
0.text:
mip alias
What is this?
mip alias allows you to create custom shortcuts for MIP commands. Stop typing long commands - use your own abbreviations and speed up your workflow.

Syntax
mip alias <subcommand> [options]

Subcommands



Subcommand
Description




set <name> <command>
Create or update an alias


remove <name>
Remove an alias


list
Show all configured aliases


clear
Remove all aliases


help
Show this help



How it works

Aliases are stored in ~/.mip/aliases.yml (global, not project-specific)
Resolution happens before command execution - MIP checks if the command matches any alias
Supports spaces in alias names (use quotes)
Supports arguments - everything after the alias is passed to the target command
Longest match - if multiple aliases match, the longest one is used

Examples

Basic alias# Create shortcut for install
mip alias set i install

# Use it
mip i lodash

Output:
✅ Alias set: i → install
   Stored in: ~/.mip/aliases.yml

== tab “Alias with spaces”
# Create alias with spaces in name
mip alias set "mip pe calc" "mip run calc"

# Use it
mip pe calc 2 plus 2

Output:
🧮 2 + 2 = 4

:::
== tab “Alias with arguments”
# Create alias for build with flags
mip alias set "mip build fast" "mip run build --mode production"

# Use it
mip build fast
# → mip run build --mode production

:::
== tab “List all aliases”
mip alias list

Output:
📋 MIP Aliases:

  i          →  install
  up         →  update
  "mip pe calc"  →  mip run calc

💡 Total: 3 aliases
📁 File: ~/.mip/aliases.yml

:::
== tab “Remove alias”
# Remove by name
mip alias remove i

Output:
✅ Alias removed: i (was → install)

Remove alias with spaces:
mip alias remove "mip pe calc"
# or
mip alias remove mip pe calc

:::
== tab “Clear all aliases”
mip alias clear

Output:
⚠️ This will remove all 3 aliases
   Are you sure? (y/N) y
✅ Cleared all 3 aliases

:::
:::
Aliases file format
Aliases are stored in ~/.mip/aliases.yml:
i: install
up: update
"mip pe calc": "mip run calc"
"mip build fast": "mip run build --mode production"

You can edit this file directly - MIP will load changes on the next run.
Common use cases
Shorten everyday commands
mip alias set i install
mip alias set up update
mip alias set ls list
mip alias set rm uninstall
mip alias set s search
mip alias set st status
mip alias set ci "mip ci --frozen-lockfile"

Shortcuts for plugin commands
mip alias set "build" "mip pe mip-webpack build"
mip alias set "dev" "mip pe mip-webpack dev"

Complex commands
# Deploy with multiple steps
mip alias set "deploy" "mip run build && mip run test && mip run deploy"

# Clean install
mip alias set "fresh" "mip cache clean && mip install"

Common errors


  
  
  

❌ ERR_ALIAS_NOT_FOUND
Cause: The specified alias doesn’t exist.
Resolution:

Check the exact name: mip alias list
Aliases with spaces need quotes: mip alias remove "my alias"
Make sure you typed the name correctly




  
  
  
  

❌ ERR_RESERVED_NAME
Cause: The alias name conflicts with a reserved MIP command.
Resolution:

Reserved names cannot be aliased: alias, config, registry, server, publish, help
Choose a different name for your alias




  
  
  

⚠️ ERR_ALIAS_OVERWRITE
Cause: An alias with the same name already exists.
Resolution:

You’ll be prompted to confirm overwrite
Type y to overwrite, n to cancel
Or use mip alias remove <name> first, then set it again


How alias resolution works
When you run a command, MIP checks:

Exact match - if the entire command matches an alias name
Longest match - if multiple aliases match, the longest one wins
Argument passing - remaining arguments are passed to the target command

Example:
Aliases:
  "build" → "mip run build"
  "build fast" → "mip run build --production"

Input: mip build fast --force
Match: "build fast" (longest match)
Result: mip run build --production --force

Related commands

mip config - manage MIP configuration
mip plugin - manage plugins (which often provide additional commands)
mip pe - execute plugin commands (often used in aliases)


        
            🔧 Aliases vs Shell Aliases
            
        
        
MIP aliases work inside MIP commands and are portable across any shell.
Shell aliases (in .bashrc or .zshrc) work at the shell level:
# Shell alias (in .zshrc)
alias mip='mip --super'

# MIP alias (stored in ~/.mip/aliases.yml)
mip alias set i install

When to use each:

Shell aliases - for global MIP flags (--super, --debug)
MIP aliases - for command substitutions (i → install)




  
  
  

💡 Pro tip: Share aliases
Copy your ~/.mip/aliases.yml file to other machines to keep your shortcuts consistent across all your development environments.

14.title: mip audit - dependency security check
14.text:
mip audit


  

What is this?
mip audit scans all installed packages and their dependencies, comparing them against a vulnerability database (GitHub Advisory Database, npm Security). You receive a report of critical, high, moderate, and low vulnerabilities, along with upgrade recommendations.

Syntax
mip audit [--fix]

Options



Flag
Description




--fix
Automatically update vulnerable packages to safe versions (if possible)



How it works

Read the lockfile - the command checks whether mip-lock.yml exists in the current directory. If it’s missing, execution stops.

Scan dependencies - for each package from the lockfile, the command sends a request to the npm registry to get a list of known vulnerabilities.

Filtering - it checks whether the current package version matches vulnerable_versions from the vulnerability database.

Sorting - all found vulnerabilities are sorted by CVSS score (from most critical to least dangerous).

Output report - vulnerabilities are grouped by severity: 🔴 Critical, 🟠 High, 🟡 Moderate, 🔵 Low.


If --fix is specified:
6. Find fixes - for each vulnerability, it checks whether patched_versions exists.
7. Update packages - runs mip install <pkg>@<fixed-version>.
8. Sync mip.yml - automatically updates mip.yml.
Examples

Standard checkmip audit

It prints a list of all vulnerabilities, including:

Package name and version
Severity level and description
CVSS score
Available patches or a recommendation to update manually
A link to the detailed vulnerability description

Example output:
🔍 Scanning dependencies... (42 packages)

🔴 lodash@4.17.19 (critical)
   Prototype Pollution vulnerability
   CVSS: 9.8
   Fix: 4.17.20
   https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-xxx

🟠 axios@0.21.1 (high)
   Server-Side Request Forgery
   CVSS: 7.5
   Fix: 0.21.2
   https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-yyy

📊 Summary:
   🔴 Critical: 1
   🟠 High: 1
   🟡 Moderate: 0
   🔵 Low: 0

💡 Run 'mip audit --fix' to auto-fix updateable vulnerabilities.

== tab “Auto-fix”
mip audit --fix


Finds all vulnerable packages.
For each vulnerability, checks that a patched version exists within the same current major release (to avoid breaking changes).
Automatically updates the package and syncs mip.yml.
Reports which packages were updated.
:::
:::

Common errors


  
  
  

❌ ERR_NO_CONNECTION
Cause: Failed to connect to the npm registry (network issues or the registry is unavailable).
Resolution:

Check your internet connection.
Make sure the npm registry is reachable.
If you’re using a proxy, configure environment variables.




  
  
  
  

❌ lock_not_found
Cause: mip-lock.yml is missing in the current directory.
Resolution:

Make sure you’re in the project root with mip.yml.
Run mip install to create the lockfile.
Try again: mip audit.




  
  
  

⚠️ update_failed
Cause: Could not install the patched package version (dependency conflicts or registry error).
Resolution:

Check whether the specified package version exists in the registry.
Try installing the patch manually: mip install <pkg>@<version>.
Check for conflicts with other dependencies.




  
  
  

🔄 Auto-fix only within the same major release
mip audit --fix will never upgrade a package to a version with a different major number (for example, 1.x → 2.x). This prevents potential breaking changes. If a safe version requires changing the major, the command will ask you to update manually.

Related commands

mip outdated - shows outdated packages (not only vulnerabilities)
mip update - updates all packages to the latest versions
mip doctor - checks the overall project health (including dependency conflicts)


        
            🔧 How patch search works
            
        
        
When --fix is enabled, the command:

Checks whether patched_versions exists.
Parses the list of available patches.
Selects the minimum version that is higher than the current one within the same major release.
Runs mip install <pkg>@<version>.
Updates mip.yml.

This approach guarantees minimal changes and a safe update.

22.title: mip cache - Manage package cache
22.text:
mip cache


  
  
  

What is this?
mip cache manages the cache of downloaded packages. MIP stores downloaded packages in two places: a local cache (.mip/ inside the project directory) and a global cache (~/.mip/store/). This command lets you check their size, clear them, or see which packages are currently used.

Syntax
mip cache <action> [--global]

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




action
string
✅
Cache action: size, clean, or usage



Options



Flag
Description




--global
Applies the action to the global cache (~/.mip/store/)



Available actions



Action
Description




size
Shows the cache size (local or global) and the number of packages in the manifest


clean
Completely removes the cache, freeing space


usage
Shows which packages from the global cache are used in the current project



How it works
size

Local cache - scans the .mip/ folder in the current project and shows its size.
Global cache (with --global) - scans ~/.mip/store/ and shows the total size of all packages.
Manifest - shows the number of packages installed in the current project.

clean

Local cache - deletes the .mip/ folder in the current project.
Global cache (with --global) - deletes all packages from ~/.mip/store/.

usage

Scans the global cache (~/.mip/store/).
Compares it with the current project’s manifest.
Shows which packages are used (📋) and which can be safely removed (💀).

Examples

View local cachemip cache size

Example output:
📦 Manifest: 3 packages
📦 Cache size: 42.50 MB

== tab “View global cache”
mip cache size --global

Example output:
🌍 Global cache size: 156.30 MB

:::
== tab “Clear local cache”
mip cache clean

Example output:
🧹 Cache cleaned: 42.50 MB freed

:::
== tab “Clear global cache”
mip cache clean --global

Example output:
🌍 Global cache cleaned: 156.30 MB freed

:::
== tab “View global cache usage”
mip cache usage --global

Example output:
📊 Global cache usage
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  📋 lodash@4.17.21  21.50 MB
  📋 express@4.18.2  25.00 MB
  💀 jest@29.7.0    32.00 MB
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
📦 Total: 78.50 MB

Legend:
  📋 - used in current project
  💀 - not used (can be cleaned)

:::
:::
Common errors


  
  
  

⚠️ No lockfile or manifest found
Cause: This project has no installed packages.
Resolution: This is not an error - there’s simply nothing to clean. Install packages via mip install.



  
  
  

⚠️ No global cache found
Cause: The global cache hasn’t been created yet (no packages were installed).
Resolution: Start installing packages - the cache will be created automatically.



  
  
  

💡 Tip
Clear the cache if:

You’re running out of disk space.
You suspect cache corruption (weird install errors).
You switched between projects that use different package versions.

Use mip cache usage --global to analyze which packages can be safely removed.

Where the cache is stored



Type
Path
Purpose




Local
.mip/
Cache for the current project (tarballs and temporary files)


Global
~/.mip/store/
Stores all package versions for all projects



Related commands

mip install - installs packages and creates the cache
mip doctor - checks project status, including cache integrity


        
            🧠 Why two caches?
            
        
        
MIP uses two cache levels to balance speed and disk space:

Global cache (~/.mip/store/) - stores all versions of all packages you’ve ever installed in any project. This saves traffic and space: if a package already exists globally, MIP just creates a symlink in the project.

Local cache (.mip/ inside the project) - stores only tarballs for fast re-installs and temporary files. It does not duplicate packages.


Cleaning the global cache removes packages from ~/.mip/store/. On the next install they will be downloaded again, but it frees a lot of disk space.

36.title: mip ci - install for CI/CD environments
36.text:
mip ci


  
  
  
  

What is this?
mip ci is a special command for non-interactive environments (CI/CD, Docker, testing servers). It installs dependencies strictly according to mip-lock.yml, ignoring mip.yml, which guarantees a reproducible installation. Unlike mip install, this command does not update the lockfile and does not check for updates - it only installs what is already locked.

Syntax
mip ci [--frozen-lockfile]

Options



Flag
Description




--frozen-lockfile
Checks that mip-lock.yml is in sync with mip.yml. If there are discrepancies, installation stops with an error.



How it works

Check that required files exist - the command verifies mip.yml and mip-lock.yml are present. If any are missing, it exits with error code 1.

Frozen lockfile check (if the flag is set) - compares dependencies from mip.yml and mip-lock.yml:

All dependencies from mip.yml must be present in the lockfile.
The lockfile must not contain extra packages that are not in mip.yml.
If discrepancies are found, it prints the problems and instructions on how to fix them.


Install from the lockfile - for each package from mip-lock.yml:

Checks whether it exists in the global cache ~/.mip/store/.
If it’s missing, it’s downloaded and extracted.
A symlink is created in node_modules/.


Progress indication - shows a progress bar with the completion percentage.


Examples

Standard CI installationmip ci

Installs all dependencies from mip-lock.yml. If the lockfile is missing, it prints an error.
Example output:
🚀 Running CI installation...
📦 Installing from lockfile (42 packages)
⬇️  Downloading lodash@4.17.21...
   ████████████████████████████████████ 100% 42/42
✅ Installed 42 packages
🔒 Lockfile integrity verified

== tab “Strict frozen lockfile check”
mip ci --frozen-lockfile

Before installation, it checks that the lockfile matches mip.yml. If there’s a mismatch, it prints an error and a hint.
Example error:
❌ Frozen lockfile check failed:
  • lodash: missing from lockfile
  • axios: extra in lockfile
💡 Run 'mip install' to update lockfile

:::
Common errors


  
  
  
  

❌ Lockfile not found
Cause: mip-lock.yml is missing in the project.
Resolution: CI must run only with a committed lockfile. Run mip install locally and commit mip-lock.yml to your repository. Never ignore the lockfile in .gitignore.



  
  
  
  

❌ mip.yml not found
Cause: mip.yml does not exist in the current directory.
Resolution: Make sure you’re in the project root. If this is a new project, create mip.yml with mip init.



  
  
  

🔒 Frozen lockfile mismatch
Cause: mip.yml was changed, but mip-lock.yml wasn’t updated.
Resolution:

Run mip install locally to sync the lockfile.
Commit the updated mip-lock.yml.
Restart CI.

To automate this, add mip ci --frozen-lockfile to your CI pipeline - this prevents people from forgetting to update the lockfile.



  
  
  

💡 Tip
Always use mip ci instead of mip install in CI/CD. It’s:

Faster - it doesn’t check for updates, it only installs.
More reliable - guarantees reproducible builds.
Safer - prevents accidental dependency updates.

The command also uses a progress bar to make the process visible.

Related commands

mip install - interactive install that updates the lockfile.
mip genlock - generates the lockfile.
mip doctor - checks overall project health.


        
            🧠 Why ci and install are different?
            
        
        
MIP splits these commands to prevent common CI mistakes:



Command
Purpose
Updates lockfile
Checks updates




mip install
Interactive install (for development)
✅ Yes
✅ Yes


mip ci
CI/CD install (for automation)
❌ No
❌ No



In CI you always want to install exactly the versions that are locked in the lockfile. Using mip install in CI can lead to nondeterministic builds if someone publishes a newer package version.
Additionally, --frozen-lockfile acts as a human error protection: if a developer forgot to update the lockfile after changing mip.yml, CI will fail with a clear error and a hint about what to do.

44.title: mip config - manage MIP configuration
44.text:
mip config
What is this?
mip config provides a convenient CLI interface for managing your MIP project configuration. Instead of manually editing mip.yml or mip.json, you can view, set, delete, and reset configuration values directly from the terminal. Perfect for quick tweaks, CI/CD automation, and scripting.

Syntax
mip config <subcommand> [options]

Subcommands



Subcommand
Description




list
Show all configuration in a formatted output


get <key>
Get a specific configuration value


set <key> <value>
Set a configuration value


delete <key>
Delete a configuration value


reset
Reset configuration to defaults


edit
Open configuration in your editor



Options



Flag
Description




--json
Output in JSON format


--yaml
Output in YAML format



How it works

Configuration detection - MIP automatically detects your config file (mip.yml, mip.json, or package.json).

Value resolution - supports nested keys using dot notation (e.g., registries.github.url).

Type detection - values are automatically parsed (strings, numbers, JSON objects, arrays).

File migration - if you’re using a legacy config format, MIP will automatically migrate to mip.yml.

Editor integration - uses $EDITOR or $VISUAL environment variables for the edit subcommand.


Examples

View all configurationmip config list

Output:
📋 MIP Configuration

   File: mip.yml
   Path: /home/user/my-project/mip.yml

📦 Project:
   name: my-awesome-app
   version: 2.0.0

📦 Dependencies:
   dependencies: 17 package(s)
   devDependencies: 5 package(s)

📜 Scripts:
   build: webpack --mode production
   dev: webpack serve
   test: jest

📦 Registries:
   default: npm
   github:
     url: https://npm.pkg.github.com/
     token: ✓ ghp_xxx…

💡 Use "mip config get <key>" to view specific values
   "mip config set <key> <value>" to update values

== tab “Get a specific value”
mip config get version

Output:
2.0.0

mip config get scripts.build

Output:
webpack --mode production

mip config get registries.github

Output:
{
  "url": "https://npm.pkg.github.com/",
  "token": "ghp_xxx"
}

:::
== tab “Set a value”
mip config set version 2.1.0

Output:
✅ Config updated: version
   Old: "2.0.0"
   New: "2.1.0"

mip config set defaultRegistry github

Output:
✅ Config updated: defaultRegistry
   Old: "npm"
   New: "github"

mip config set registries.github.token ghp_new_token

Output:
✅ Config updated: registries.github.token
   Old: "ghp_xxx"
   New: "ghp_new_token"

:::
== tab “Delete a value”
mip config delete registries.github.token

Output:
✅ Deleted: registries.github.token
   Old value: "ghp_xxx"

:::
== tab “Edit in editor”
mip config edit

Output:
📝 Opening mip.yml in vim...
✅ Config edited successfully

:::
== tab “Reset to defaults”
mip config reset

Output:
⚠️ This will reset all configuration to default values
   All custom settings will be lost!
   Are you sure? (yes/no) yes
✅ Config reset to defaults

:::
:::
Common use cases
Quick version bump
# Before publishing
mip config set version 2.1.0
mip config get version  # Verify

Registry management
# Set default registry
mip config set defaultRegistry github

# Update registry token
mip config set registries.github.token ghp_new_token

# Add a new registry
mip config set registries.gitlab.url https://gitlab.com/api/v4/packages/npm/

Script management
# Add a new script
mip config set scripts.deploy "npm run build && rsync -avz dist/ server:/app"

# Update an existing script
mip config set scripts.test "jest --coverage"

Workspace management
# Set workspaces
mip config set workspaces '["packages/*", "apps/*"]'

Supported keys



Key
Type
Description




name
string
Project name


version
string
Project version


description
string
Project description


dependencies
object
Production dependencies


devDependencies
object
Development dependencies


scripts
object
NPM scripts


workspaces
array
Monorepo workspaces


registries
object
Package registries


defaultRegistry
string
Default registry name


language
string
UI language (en/ru/etc)



Nested keys
You can access nested values using dot notation:
mip config get registries.github.url
mip config set registries.github.token ghp_xxx
mip config delete registries.github.token

Output formats
JSON format
mip config list --json

Useful for scripting and programmatic access.
YAML format
mip config list --yaml

Shows the raw YAML configuration.
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ ERR_NO_CONFIG
Cause: No config file found in the current directory.
Resolution:

Make sure you’re in a MIP project directory.
Run mip init to create a new project.
Or run mip config from the project root.




  
  
  
  

❌ ERR_KEY_NOT_FOUND
Cause: The specified key doesn’t exist in the configuration.
Resolution:

Check the key name: mip config list to see all

63.title: mip create - create a new project from a template
63.text:
mip create


  
  
  
  

What is this?
mip create is a fast way to start a new project. It generates a project folder, creates all the required files based on the selected template, and automatically installs dependencies via mip install. This saves you from manually copying boilerplate code.

Syntax
mip create <template> <project-name>

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




template
string
✅
Template name: node, react, cli, express


project-name
string
✅
Project name (used as the folder name and as name in mip.yml)



Available templates



Template
Description
Installed packages




node
A basic Node.js project with index.js and start/dev scripts
nodemon


react
A React application with Webpack configured via react-scripts
Installed via npm (for compatibility)


cli
A CLI utility template with an executable file in bin/
None


express
An Express server with routes and start/dev scripts
express, nodemon



How it works

Validate arguments - if template or project-name is missing, it prints help.

Load the template - selects the configuration from the templates object for the provided template name.

Create the project folder - if a folder with that name already exists, the operation stops.

Generate files - all template files are created inside the project folder:

Supports placeholder substitution {{name}} → replaced with project-name.
Creates nested folders recursively.


Set file permissions - for the cli template, bin/cli.js is marked as executable (chmod 755).

Install dependencies - if the template includes a list of packages, it runs mip install for each one.


Examples

Create a Node.js projectmip create node my-app

Result:
📦 Creating node project: my-app

  ✅ index.js
  ✅ package.json
  ✅ README.md
  ✅ .gitignore

📦 Installing dependencies...
  ✅ nodemon installed

✅ Created! cd my-app && mip run start

Project structure:
my-app/
├── index.js          # Main file
├── package.json      # Dependencies and scripts
├── README.md         # Documentation

72.title: mip dedupe - remove duplicate dependencies
72.text:
mip dedupe


  
  
  
  
  

What is this?
mip dedupe finds and removes duplicate package versions in your project. This helps reduce size, speed up installation, and avoid version conflicts. The command has two modes: quick (default) and full - for maximum optimization.

Syntax
mip dedupe [--full]

Options



Flag
Description




--full
Enables full deduplication mode - more aggressively rebuilds the dependency graph, removing all possible duplicates. Can take longer.



How it works

Analyze the lockfile - reads mip-lock.yml and builds a dependency graph.

Find duplicates - identifies packages where:

The package name is the same.
Versions are different.
Both versions satisfy requirements of all parent packages.


Check compatibility - determines whether the old version can be replaced with the new one without breaking compatibility (it checks the major version).

Report results - shows the list of found duplicates, including:

Package name and versions.
Which versions can be deleted (compatible).
Which versions must be kept (incompatible).


Ask for confirmation - before making changes, the command asks: “Do you want to deduplicate? (y/N)”.

Run deduplication:

Quick mode - removes only compatible duplicates (within the same major version).
Full mode (--full) - removes all duplicates, even if major versions differ (may cause breaking changes).


Update the lockfile - after deletion, updates mip-lock.yml.

Show the result - reports how many packages were removed and how many remain.


Examples

Quick deduplicationmip dedupe

Example report:
📊 Duplicate packages found:

  📦 lodash
     ✅ Keeping: 4.17.21
     🔄 Compatible (can dedupe):
        - 4.17.19

  📦 axios
     ✅ Keeping: 0.21.4
     🔄 Compatible (can dedupe):
        - 0.21.1
     ⚠️  Incompatible (keep both):
        - 0.19.2

Do you want to deduplicate? (y/N) y

🧹 Running deduplication...
✅ Deduplication complete!
   Removed: 3 packages
   Remaining: 42 packages

Removed packages:
  • lodash@4.17.19 → kept 4.17.21
  • axios@0.21.1 → kept 0.21.4

== tab “Full deduplication”
mip dedupe --full

Analyzes all possible version combinations and максимально compacts the dependency graph. Recommended after large updates.
Example report:
📊 Duplicate packages found:
  ...
Do you want to deduplicate? (y/N) y

🧹 Running full deduplication...
✅ Full deduplication complete!
   Removed: 8 packages
   Remaining: 56 packages

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ No duplicates found
Cause: Your dependency tree is already optimized.
Resolution: This is not an error - everything is fine. Continue your work.



  
  
  

❌ Lockfile not found
Cause: mip-lock.yml is missing.
Resolution:

Run mip install to create the lockfile.
Then run mip dedupe again.




  
  
  

💡 When to use dedupe

After updating many packages.
Before building a production image (to reduce its size).
When you suspect version conflicts at runtime.

It’s recommended to run mip dedupe before committing, to keep the project clean and reduce CI/CD time.

Related commands

mip install - installs dependencies and creates the lockfile.
mip outdated - shows outdated packages (often duplicates appear due to outdated versions).
mip doctor - checks overall project health.


        
            🧠 What is deduplication?
            
        
        
In package managers, duplication happens when multiple packages require different versions of the same dependency. For example, if package A requires lodash@4.17.19 and package B requires lodash@4.17.21, MIP will install both versions.
Quick mode (dedupe without flags):

Checks only compatible versions (within the same major).
Removes duplicates that can be safely replaced with one shared version.
Works fast.

Full mode (dedupe --full):

Scans the whole dependency tree.
Rebuilds it to maximize shared versions.
May take longer, but gives a better result.

Important: deduplication never violates package requirements - a version is replaced only if it’s compatible with all parent packages.

80.title: mip doctor - diagnose the environment and the project
80.text:
mip doctor


  
  
  
  
  

What is this?
mip doctor is a diagnostic utility that checks your environment for potential problems. It analyzes tool versions, network availability, access permissions, and PATH configuration-helping you quickly identify and fix common issues before installing packages.

Syntax
mip doctor

How it works

Check Node.js - analyzes the current Node.js version. Recommended version is v18 or higher.

Check MIP - displays the currently installed MIP version.

Check the global cache (~/.mip/) - verifies that the global storage exists and checks its size.

Check configuration - verifies that mip.yml exists and outputs the number of dependencies, scripts, and workspaces.

Check the manifest - checks .mip/manifest.json and validates that package paths are intact.

Check the local .mip/ directory - checks that the local cache exists.

Check PATH - ensures that MIP global binaries (~/.mip/global/node_modules/.bin) are present in PATH.

Check Git - verifies that Git is available (required for GitHub packages).

Check network - tries to reach the npm registry (registry.npmjs.org).

Check disk - verifies write permissions in a temporary directory.

Report results - shows all found issues and suggests how to fix them.


Examples

Healthy environmentmip doctor

Sample output:
🔍 Running diagnostics...

✅ Node.js v20.11.0
✅ mip v2.0
✅ ~/.mip exists
✅ Global store: 42.50 MB
✅ Config: mip.yml
   📦 3 dependencies
   📜 2 scripts
   📁 1 workspace(s)
✅ Manifest: 3 package(s) installed
   ✅ All package paths exist
✅ Local .mip exists
✅ Global bin in PATH
✅ Git installed
✅ Network: npm registry reachable
✅ Disk writeable

📊 Summary: 0 warning(s)

✨ System is healthy!

== tab “Problematic environment”
mip doctor

Sample output:
🔍 Running diagnostics...

⚠️ Node.js v12.22.0 (recommended: v18+)
✅ mip v2.0
⚠️ ~/.mip directory missing (will be created on first install)
ℹ️ No config file found (mip.yml, mip.json, or package.json)
⚠️ Global bin not in PATH: /home/user/.mip/global/node_modules/.bin
⚠️ Git not found (required for GitHub packages)
⚠️ Cannot reach npm registry (check network)
✅ Disk writeable

📊 Summary: 5 warning(s)

Issues found:

  ⚠️ Node.js v12.22.0 (recommended: v18+)
  ⚠️ ~/.mip directory missing
  ⚠️ Global bin not in PATH
  ⚠️ Git not found
  ⚠️ Cannot reach npm registry

💡 Fix suggestions:
  • Add to ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc:
    export PATH="$PATH:/home/user/.mip/global/node_modules/.bin"
  • Install git: https://git-scm.com/
  • Run "mip init" to create a config file

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

⚠️ Node.js version too old
Cause: An outdated Node.js version is installed (below v14).
Resolution:

Use nvm to install a current version:nvm install 20
nvm use 20


Or download the latest version from nodejs.org.




  
  
  

⚠️ Global bin not in PATH
Cause: MIP global binaries are not added to the environment variable PATH.
Resolution: Add to your ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc:
export PATH="$PATH:~/.mip/global/node_modules/.bin"

Then restart your shell: source ~/.zshrc



  
  
  
  

⚠️ Cannot reach npm registry
Cause: Network problems or the npm registry is unreachable.
Resolution:

Check your internet connection.
Check your proxy settings.
Temporarily disable VPN/firewall.
Make sure DNS works correctly.




  
  
  

⚠️ Git not found
Cause: Git is not installed or is missing from PATH.
Resolution:

Linux/Mac: sudo apt install git or brew install git
Windows: Download from git-scm.com


Related commands

mip install - installs dependencies (after running doctor).
mip cache clean - clears the cache if you run into disk-related issues.
mip ci - CI/CD installation (doctor helps you pre-check the environment).


        
            🧠 Why is doctor important?
            
        
        
mip doctor is a first line of defense against common problems:

Saves time - instead of guessing why installation fails, you get a clear diagnosis.
Prevents mistakes - many issues (e.g., outdated Node.js) lead to confusing errors that are hard to interpret.
Improves onboarding - new developers only need to run mip doctor to understand what must be configured in their environment.

Tip: Add mip doctor to your README.md as the very first step after cloning the repository.

87.title: mip exec - execute binaries from dependencies
87.text:
mip exec


  
  

What is this?
mip exec runs executable files shipped by your dependencies. It does not require you to install those binaries globally-MIP finds them in the installed packages and executes them.

Syntax
mip exec <command> [-- <args>...]

Arguments

<command> - the binary name to run (for example: jest, eslint, ts-node).
[-- <args>...] - optional arguments passed to the binary.

How it works

MIP reads the installed package manifests to find where the requested binary lives.
It resolves the correct path for the current project.
Then it executes the binary with the provided arguments.

Examples

Run a binarymip exec jest

Runs Jest from the project dependencies.
== tab “Pass arguments”
mip exec eslint -- .

Executes eslint and passes -- . as-is.
:::
== tab “Run with a specific package version (if applicable)”
mip exec <binary>

MIP resolves the binary based on the installed packages for the current project.
:::
:::
Related commands

mip run - run scripts defined in mip.yml.
mip install - install dependencies and update mip.yml/lockfile.

94.title: mip exports - view package export paths
94.text:
mip exports


  
  
  

What is this?
mip exports is a utility for viewing the exported paths (exports) of a package. It shows which files and subpaths the package makes available for import. This is especially useful when working with packages that use exports in package.json to control their public API, or when you want to know where to import specific files (e.g., lodash/fp, react-dom/server).

Syntax
mip exports <package-name>

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




package-name
string
✅
The name of the package whose exports you want to view



How it works

Determine project directory - the command gets the current working directory.

Find the package - via ExportsResolver, the command locates the installed package in the local .mip/ cache.

Read exports - parses the exports section in the package’s package.json (if it exists).

Check availability - for each exported path, checks whether the file physically exists.

Output results - displays a list of all available paths with an indicator of file existence (✅ or ❌).


Examples

View React exportsmip exports react

Example output:
📦 Exports for react:
  ✅ .
  ✅ index.js
  ✅ jsx-dev-runtime
  ✅ jsx-runtime
  ✅ react.shared-subset
  ✅ server
  ✅ server.browser
  ✅ server.node

== tab “View Lodash exports”
mip exports lodash

Example output:
📦 Exports for lodash:
  ✅ .
  ✅ add
  ✅ array
  ✅ chain
  ✅ collection
  ✅ date
  ✅ fp
  ✅ function
  ✅ lang
  ✅ math
  ✅ number
  ✅ object
  ✅ seq
  ✅ string
  ✅ util

:::
== tab “Package with no exports”
mip exports some-old-package

Example output:
❌ No exports found for some-old-package

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ Package not found
Cause: The specified package is not installed in the project.
Solution:

Check the list of installed packages: mip list
Install the package: mip install <package-name>
Try again: mip exports <package-name>




  
  
  

💡 Tip
mip exports is especially useful for:

Exploring a new package - to understand what can be imported.
Migration - when switching from CommonJS to ESM, when the package changes its export structure.
Working with monorepos - to understand which subpackages are available.

Use this command before writing imports - it will show you which paths actually exist.

Related commands

mip info - shows package information (version, description, dependencies).
mip list - shows all installed packages.
mip install - installs packages.


        
            🧠 What is exports in package.json?
            
        
        
The exports field in package.json is a modern standard for controlling the public API of a package. It defines:

Which files are available - not all files in the package are public, only those listed in exports.
How they are available - you can configure different paths for require and import.
Conditional exports - different versions for Node.js, browser, React Native, etc.

Example from react/package.json:
{
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "require": "./index.js",
      "import": "./index.js"
    },
    "./jsx-runtime": {
      "require": "./jsx-runtime.js",
      "import": "./jsx-runtime.js"
    },
    "./server": {
      "require": "./server.node.js",
      "import": "./server.browser.js"
    }
  }
}

mip exports shows all possible paths that the package exports, with verification that the files actually exist.


        
            🔍 Why are some paths marked ❌?
            
        
        
A path may be marked ❌ if:

There is an entry in exports, but the file is physically missing (package error).
The export is described with conditions (browser, node, default), and it is not available in your environment.

In any case, if a path is marked ❌ - the import will not work. mip exports helps identify such issues before writing code.

102.title: mip genlock - generate lockfile
102.text:
mip genlock


  
  

What is this?
mip genlock generates a lockfile (mip-lock.yml) based on the dependencies specified in mip.yml. Unlike mip install, this command does not install packages locally - it only resolves their versions and creates a file with pinned versions, URLs, and dependencies. This is useful for pre-generating a lockfile in CI/CD or after manually updating mip.yml.

Syntax
mip genlock

How it works

Check for mip.yml - the command checks if mip.yml exists in the current directory. If not, execution stops.

Read dependencies - extracts dependencies and devDependencies sections from mip.yml.

Resolve versions - for each dependency (name + version range), queries the npm registry to get the exact latest version that satisfies the range.

Collect metadata - for each package, fetches:

Exact version (version).
Tarball URL (resolved).
List of dependencies (dependencies).
List of peer dependencies (peerDependencies).


Generate lockfile - creates mip-lock.yml with the following structure:
version: 1.0.0
generatedAt: '2026-01-15T14:23:00.000Z'
packages:
  lodash@4.17.21:
    version: 4.17.21
    resolved: https://registry.npmjs.org/lodash/-/lodash-4.17.21.tgz
    dependencies: {}
    peerDependencies: {}
    installPath: .mip/lodash/4.17.21


Output results - shows how many packages were successfully added to the lockfile.


Examples

Generate lockfilemip genlock

Example output:
🔒 Generating lockfile...
  ⏳ Resolving packages... 1/42
  ⏳ Resolving packages... 42/42

✅ Generated mip-lock.yml with 42 packages

== tab “When mip.yml is empty”
mip genlock

Example output:
ℹ️ No dependencies found in mip.yml

:::
== tab “On resolve error”
mip genlock

Example output:
🔒 Generating lockfile...
  ⏳ Resolving packages... 1/42
  ❌ Failed to resolve some-package@^2.0.0: Package not found

✅ Generated mip-lock.yml with 41 packages

:::
Common errors


  
  
  
  

❌ mip.yml not found
Cause: No mip.yml file in the current directory.
Solution:

Make sure you are in the project root.
If it’s a new project - create mip.yml with mip init.
Or use mip create to generate a ready-made project.




  
  
  

❌ No dependencies found
Cause: No dependencies specified in mip.yml (empty dependencies and devDependencies sections).
Solution: This is not an error, just a warning. If you want to pin dependencies - add them to mip.yml and run mip genlock again.



  
  
  

❌ Failed to resolve package
Cause: Package not found in the registry or the specified version does not exist.
Solution:

Check the package name and version in mip.yml.
Check your internet connection.
If the package is private - make sure the correct registries are configured.
After fixing, run mip genlock again.




  
  
  

💡 Tip
mip genlock requires registry access, as it resolves versions in real time.
It is recommended to use mip genlock:

After manually editing mip.yml.
In CI/CD to generate a lockfile from mip.yml without installing packages.
In automation scripts to check dependency availability.


Related commands

mip install - installs dependencies and generates a lockfile.
mip ci - installs dependencies from the lockfile.
mip update - updates dependencies and regenerates the lockfile.


        
            🧠 Why do you need a lockfile?
            
        
        
A lockfile is a snapshot of the dependency tree at a specific point in time. It guarantees:

Reproducibility - all developers and CI get identical package versions.
Speed - no need to resolve dependencies on every install.
Security - if a package is removed from the registry, you still have the pinned version.

Difference between mip install and mip genlock:



Command
Installs packages to global cache
Generates lockfile




mip install
✅ Yes
✅ Yes (if no lockfile)


mip genlock
❌ No
✅ Yes (always)



mip genlock allows you to prepare a lockfile in advance in CI, without spending time on installing packages.


        
            🔧 mip-lock.yml format
            
        
        
version: 1.0.0
generatedAt: '2026-01-15T14:23:00.000Z'
packages:
  lodash@4.17.21:
    version: 4.17.21
    resolved: https://registry.npmjs.org/lodash/-/lodash-4.17.21.tgz
    dependencies: {}
    peerDependencies: {}
    installPath: .mip/lodash/4.17.21
  react@18.2.0:
    version: 18.2.0
    resolved: https://registry.npmjs.org/react/-/react-18.2.0.tgz
    dependencies:
      loose-envify: ^1.1.0
      scheduler: ^0.23.0
    peerDependencies: {}
    installPath: .mip/react/18.2.0

Fields:

version - lockfile format version (for future compatibility).
generatedAt - generation time (ISO 8601).
packages - object with keys in the format name@version and metadata.
resolved - URL for downloading the tarball.
installPath - path where the package will be installed (relative to project root).

109.title: mip global - manage global packages
109.text:
mip global


  
  
  

What is this?
mip global is a command for managing globally installed packages. Unlike regular installation (which uses the global cache and symlinks in the project), global installation places packages in ~/.mip/global/ and makes their binaries available from anywhere via the command line. This is convenient for tools you use across all projects, such as test runners, linters, or code generators.

Syntax
mip global <action> [package-name]

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




action
string
✅
Action: install, uninstall, list


package-name
string
❌
Package name (required for install and uninstall)



Available actions



Action
Description




install
Installs a package globally, automatically adds the binary to PATH


uninstall
Removes a globally installed package


list
Shows a list of all globally installed packages



How it works
install

Creates the global directory ~/.mip/global/ (if it doesn’t exist).
Changes to that directory.
If mip.json is missing - creates it via mip init.
Installs the specified package via mip install.
Removes the temporary mip.json (if it was created).
Checks whether the global folder (~/.mip/global/node_modules/.bin) is in PATH.
If not - adds it to .zshrc or .bashrc.

uninstall

Removes the package folder from ~/.mip/global/node_modules/.
Reports successful removal or that the package was not found.

list

Scans the ~/.mip/global/node_modules/ folder.
Outputs a list of all installed packages (excluding internal ones).

Examples

Install a global packagemip global install eslint

Example output:
🌍 Installing eslint globally...
✅ Added to PATH in /home/user/.zshrc
🔄 Run: source /home/user/.zshrc
✅ eslint installed globally
📁 Location: /home/user/.mip/global/node_modules/.bin/eslint

Now you can use eslint from anywhere:
eslint --fix src/

== tab “Install multiple packages”
mip global install jest typescript nodemon

Example output:
🌍 Installing jest globally...
✅ jest installed globally
🌍 Installing typescript globally...
✅ typescript installed globally
🌍 Installing nodemon globally...
✅ nodemon installed globally

:::
== tab “List global packages”
mip global list

Example output:
🌍 Globally installed packages:

  • eslint
  • jest
  • typescript
  • nodemon

📊 Total: 4

:::
== tab “Uninstall a global package”
mip global uninstall eslint

Example output:
🗑️ Uninstalling eslint globally...
✅ eslint removed globally

:::
== tab “Attempt to remove a non-existent package”
mip global uninstall some-package

Example output:
🗑️ Uninstalling some-package globally...
❌ some-package not found globally

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

⚠️ Package not found
Cause: The specified package does not exist in the registry.
Solution:

Check the spelling of the package name.
Make sure the package is available in the registry: mip search <package-name>




  
  
  

🔄 PATH not updated
Cause: The global folder has not been added to PATH.
Solution:
After installation, the command tries to add PATH automatically. If it doesn’t work - add it manually:
echo 'export PATH="$PATH:~/.mip/global/node_modules/.bin"' >> ~/.zshrc
source ~/.zshrc




  
  
  

💡 Tip
When to use global installation:

Tools you use across all projects (e.g., eslint, prettier, jest).
CLI utilities that should be available from anywhere (e.g., create-react-app, vue-cli).

When NOT to use global installation:

Dependencies specific to a single project.
Packages that may conflict across different project versions.

Best practice: Prefer local installations (mip install) for projects, and use global only for universal tools.

Related commands

mip install - local installation in the current project.
mip list - shows locally installed packages.
mip doctor - environment diagnostics (checks PATH).


        
            🧠 Where are global packages stored?
            
        
        
~/.mip/
├── global/
│   ├── node_modules/
│   │   ├── eslint/
│   │   ├── jest/
│   │   └── .bin/
│   │       ├── eslint        ← link to executable
│   │       └── jest          ← link to executable
│   └── mip.json (temporary)
└── store/                     (global package cache)

Structure:

~/.mip/global/ - root of the global environment.
node_modules/ - installed packages.
.bin/ - executable files (added to PATH).

Important: Global packages do not depend on the local mip.yml and are not included in mip-lock.yml.

129.title: mip info - get package information
129.text:
mip info


  
  
  

What is this?
mip info is a utility for quickly retrieving information about any package from the npm registry. It shows the description, author, versions, links to the homepage and repository, as well as the publication date. This is useful for exploring a new package before installation, checking version currency, or finding a link to the documentation.

Syntax
mip info <package-name>

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




package-name
string

How it works

Argument check - if no package name is provided, usage help is displayed.

Parallel request - two requests are made to the registry simultaneously:

Fetching information about the latest version of the package.
Fetching a list of all available versions.


Report generation - from the retrieved data, an information card is assembled:

Package name and latest version.
Description.
Author.
Homepage.
Number of available versions.
Publication date.
Repository link.


Recent versions list - shows the last 5 versions with a marker for the current one.


Examples

137.title: mip init - initialize a new project
137.text:
mip init


  
  
  
  

What is this?
mip init is the first command you run to create a new MIP project. It creates the mip.yml file, sets up the folder structure, and generates a README file with usage instructions. Everything you need to get started - in one command!

Syntax
mip init

What is created
my-project/
├── mip.yml          # Project configuration in YAML
├── .mip/
│   ├── manifest.json # Installed packages manifest
│   ├── cache/        # Download cache
│   └── packages/     # Local packages
└── README.md        # Documentation

Example mip.yml
name: my-project
version: 1.0.0
language: en
dependencies: {}
devDependencies: {}
scripts: {}
workspaces: []

How it works

Check if mip.yml exists - if the file already exists, the command exits with an error.

Check for old configs - if mip.json or package.json exists, MIP automatically migrates them to mip.yml.

Create mip.yml - a minimal configuration is automatically generated in YAML format:
name: folder-name
version: 1.0.0
language: en
dependencies: {}
devDependencies: {}
scripts: {}
workspaces: []


Create .mip directory - structure for storing cache and manifest:
.mip/
├── manifest.json   # Installed packages manifest
├── cache/          # Download cache
└── packages/       # Local packages


Check system compatibility:

Node.js version (recommended: v18+).
Write permissions to the .mip folder.


Generate README.md - if the file doesn’t exist, it is created with basic instructions.

Display final report - shows the project structure and suggests next steps.


Migration from old formats
If you have mip.json or package.json, MIP automatically migrates them on first run:
# Before
mip.json
mip-lock.json

# After
mip.yml          # with mip.json.backup
mip-lock.yml     # with mip-lock.json.backup

💡 Why YAML?
YAML is easier to read, supports comments, and is more convenient for manual editing. You can add comments directly in mip.yml:
# This is my project
name: my-project
# Project version
version: 1.0.0


Examples

Initialize a new projectmip init

Output:
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                    🚀 MIP INIT STARTED 🚀                  ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

📋 Step 1/4: Creating mip.yml...
  ✅ mip.yml created
     📄 Path: /home/user/my-project/mip.yml
     📦 Name: my-project
     🔢 Version: 1.0.0

📂 Step 2/4: Creating .mip directory...
  ✅ .mip directory created
     📁 Path: /home/user/my-project/.mip

🔧 Step 3/4: Setting up directory structure...
  ✅ Manifest: .mip/manifest.json
  ✅ Cache directory: .mip/cache/
  ✅ Temp directory: .mip/temp/

🔐 Step 4/4: Checking system compatibility...
  ✅ Node.js version: v20.11.0
  ✅ Write permissions: OK

╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║              ✅ MIP PROJECT INITIALIZED! ✅                ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

📊 Summary:
   📁 Project: my-project
   📂 Location: /home/user/my-project
   🗂️  Structure: .mip/ (cache + packages + temp)
   📄 Config: mip.yml
   📋 Manifest: .mip/manifest.json

💡 Next steps:
   1️⃣  Install packages:  mip install express
   2️⃣  Run scripts:       mip run start
   3️⃣  List packages:     mip list

== tab “Repeated run”
mip init

Output:
⚠️  mip.yml already exists

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

⚠️ mip.yml already exists
Cause: The project is already initialized.
Solution:

If you want to recreate the project - delete mip.yml and .mip/ manually, then run mip init again.
If you just want to start working - use other commands: mip install, mip list, etc.




  
  
  

⚠️ Node.js version too old
Cause: An outdated version of Node.js is installed (below v14).
Solution:

Update Node.js to version 18 or higher.
Use nvm to manage versions.




  
  
  

💡 Tip
After mip init you can immediately:

Install dependencies: mip install express
Add scripts to mip.yml:scripts:
  start: node index.js
  dev: nodemon index.js


Run a script: mip run start


Related commands

mip create - creates a project from a template (node, react, cli, express)
mip install - installs packages in the created project
mip doctor - diagnoses the environment after initialization

147.title: mip install - install dependencies
147.text:
mip install


  
  
  
  

What is this?
mip install is the main command for installing dependencies. It downloads packages from the npm registry, saves them to the global cache ~/.mip/store/, creates symlinks in node_modules/, and updates the manifest and lockfile. Supports installing multiple packages, global installation, and non-interactive mode for CI.

Syntax
mip install [packages...] [options]

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




packages
array
❌
List of packages to install. If not specified - installs all dependencies from mip.yml.



Options



Flag
Description




--save-dev / -D
Saves the package to devDependencies


--force / -f
Forces reinstallation of packages (overwrites cache)


--global / -g
Installs the package globally


--no-save
Installs the package without saving to mip.yml


--ci
Non-interactive mode (automatically skips peer dependencies)



How it works
mip install (no arguments)

Read mip.yml - loads dependencies from dependencies and devDependencies.
Check lockfile - if mip-lock.yml is missing, it will be created.
Install all packages - sequentially installs all dependencies.
Progress bar - displays installation progress.
Peer dependency resolution - in interactive mode, asks for confirmation; in --ci mode, automatically skips.

mip install <package>

Version resolution - if package@version is specified, installs that specific version; otherwise - latest.
Dependency resolution - fetches package information from the registry.
Download and extract - downloads the tarball and extracts it to the global cache ~/.mip/store/.
Create symlink - creates a symbolic link in node_modules/ pointing to the global cache.
Install package dependencies - recursively installs nested dependencies.
Update lockfile - pins exact versions in mip-lock.yml.
Save to mip.yml - adds the package to dependencies (unless --no-save is specified).
Update manifest - writes information to .mip/manifest.json.

Examples

Install all dependenciesmip install

Output:
📦 Installing all dependencies (42 packages)...

  ████████████████████████████████████ 100% 42/42

✅ Installed 42 packages in 12.3s

== tab “Install multiple packages”
mip install react electron

Installs both packages in parallel.
:::
== tab “Install a specific package”
mip install express

:::
== tab “Install a specific version”
mip install lodash@4.17.21

:::
== tab “Install and save to devDependencies”
mip install jest --save-dev

:::
== tab “CI mode (non-interactive)”
mip install --ci

Automatically skips all peer dependency prompts.
:::
== tab “Global installation”
mip install --global typescript

:::
Global cache
MIP uses a global cache at ~/.mip/store/. All packages are stored once and used across all projects:
~/.mip/store/
├── lodash/
│   └── 4.17.21/
│       ├── package.json
│       └── index.js
└── express/
    └── 4.18.2/
        ├── package.json
        └── lib/

This saves up to 90% of disk space.
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ Package not found
Cause: The specified package does not exist in the npm registry.
Solution:

Check the package name spelling.
Use mip search to find the package.




  
  
  

❌ Version not found
Cause: The specified package version does not exist.
Solution:

Check available versions: mip info <package>




  
  
  
  

⚠️ Peer dependency conflicts
Cause: The package requires a version of another package that conflicts with an already installed one.
Solution:

In interactive mode, MIP will offer options.
In CI, use --ci to automatically skip.
Manually install the required version.




  
  
  

💡 Tip

For clean installs in CI, use mip ci - it’s faster and more reliable.
Use --force only if you’re sure the cache is corrupted.
For global packages, use mip global install (more convenient interface).


Related commands

mip ci - installation for CI/CD
mip update - update packages
mip uninstall - remove packages
mip list - view installed packages
mip global - manage global packages

159.title: mip language - manage interface language
159.text:
mip language


  
  
  

What is this?
mip language is a command for managing the MIP interface language within the current project. MIP supports multi-language and automatically adapts message output to the language specified in mip.yml. This is convenient for teams with different preferences and for projects where localization matters.

Syntax
mip language [lang]

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




lang
string
❌
Language code (e.g., en, ru, de). If not specified - shows the current language.



Available languages



Code
Language




en
English (default)


ru
Русский


de
Deutsch



How it works

Determine current language - MIP checks settings in the following order:

Language from mip.yml (the language field).
System language (if not specified in the project).
en (if unable to determine).


Show current language - if lang is not specified, the command displays the current language and the list of available ones.

Set new language - if a language code is provided:

Checks whether the language is supported.
Updates mip.yml (adds or updates the language field).
Displays confirmation of successful language change.



Examples

View current languagemip language

Example output:
🌐 Current language: en
📋 Available languages: en, ru, de

== tab “Change language to Russian”
mip language ru

Example output:
✅ Language set to: ru

After this, all MIP commands will output messages in Russian.
:::
== tab “Change language to German”
mip language de

Example output:
✅ Language set to: de

:::
== tab “Change to unsupported language”
mip language fr

Example output:
❌ Invalid language: fr
📋 Available languages: en, ru, de

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ Invalid language
Cause: A language code that is not supported was specified.
Solution:

Use one of the available languages: en, ru, de.
Check the spelling of the language code.




  
  
  

💡 Tip

The language is saved per project (in mip.yml), not globally. This allows different projects to use different languages.
If you want to change the language globally - change your system settings or the LANG environment variable.
For new projects, the default language is determined from system settings.


Related commands

mip init - creates a new project with the language specified in mip.yml.
mip info - outputs package information in the selected language.


        
            🧠 How does multi-language work in MIP?
            
        
        
MIP uses a two-level localization system:

Project level - the language is stored in mip.yml (the language field). This language is used for all commands within the project.

System level - if no language is specified in the project, MIP looks at the system LANG or LC_ALL variable.


Priority:
Language in mip.yml > System LANG variable > en (default)

Translations:
All MIP messages are stored in localization files:
lib/i18n/
├── en.json  # English
├── ru.json  # Russian
└── de.json  # German

Each file contains key-value pairs for all command messages:
{
  "commands.install.installing": "Installing {package}...",
  "commands.install.installed": "✅ {package} installed"
}

When changing the language via mip language, only the field in mip.yml is updated, and the system picks up the new language on the next command execution.


        
            🔧 Adding new languages
            
        
        
If you want to add support for a new language:

Create a file in lib/i18n/<lang>.json.
Copy the structure from an existing file.
Translate all messages to the new language.
Add the language code to the languages array in lib/i18n/index.js.
Done! The language is now available via mip language <lang>.

Important: When adding a new language, make sure all messages are translated. Missing translations will fall back to English.

168.title: mip legacy - work with legacy packages
168.text:
mip legacy


  
  

What is this?
mip legacy is a utility for working with legacy packages in your project. It helps identify packages that have not been updated for a long time, contain outdated dependency formats, or require manual intervention. The command allows you to view a list of such packages, try to automatically fix them, or clean up emulation of old dependencies.

Syntax
mip legacy <action> [package-name]

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




action
string
✅
Action: list, fix, clean


package-name
string
❌
Package name (required for fix)



Available actions



Action
Description




list
Shows all legacy packages in the project


fix
Attempts to fix a legacy package (emulates dependencies)


clean
Cleans up emulations of old dependencies



How it works
list

Scans the .mip/ folder for installed packages.
For each package, checks whether it is “legacy” (according to LegacyFallback criteria).
Outputs a list of such packages with a fix hint.

fix

Takes a package name.
Runs the dependency emulation mechanism via emulateDependencies().
Outputs a report on how many dependencies were emulated.

clean

Cleans up all legacy dependency emulations.
Outputs the number of emulations removed.

Examples

List legacy packagesmip legacy list

Example output:
🔍 Checking for legacy packages...

⚠️ Found 3 legacy packages:
  • lodash@4.17.19 (deprecated)
  • request@2.88.2 (unmaintained)
  • moment@2.29.1 (legacy format)

💡 Run 'mip legacy fix <package>' to try automatic fix

== tab “Fix a legacy package”
mip legacy fix lodash

Example output:
🔧 Fixing package: lodash
✅ Emulated dependencies for lodash: 3 dependencies resolved

:::
== tab “Clean emulations”
mip legacy clean

Example output:
🧹 Cleaned emulation: 5 entries removed

:::
== tab “No legacy packages”
mip legacy list

Example output:
🔍 Checking for legacy packages...
✅ No legacy packages found

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ No packages found
Cause: No packages are installed in the project (.mip/ folder is missing or empty).
Solution:

Install packages: mip install
Then retry: mip legacy list




  
  
  

❌ Package not found for fix
Cause: The specified package is not legacy or is not installed.
Solution:

Check the list of legacy packages: mip legacy list
Make sure the package name is spelled correctly.




  
  
  

💡 Tip
Regularly run mip legacy list to track package deprecation:

Packages that haven’t been updated for over 2 years may contain vulnerabilities.
Outdated formats can slow down installation.
It is recommended to replace legacy packages with modern alternatives (e.g., request → axios).


Related commands

mip outdated - shows version-outdated packages.
mip audit - checks packages for vulnerabilities.
mip update - updates packages to the latest versions.
mip doctor - diagnoses project state.


        
            🧠 What makes a package ‘legacy’?
            
        
        
MIP defines legacy packages by several criteria:

Publication date - package has not been updated for more than 2 years.
Dependency format - uses outdated format (e.g., peerDependencies in old style).
Registry warnings - package is marked as deprecated in the npm registry.
Critical vulnerabilities - package contains vulnerabilities with no patches available.

Check example:
// Example criterion from LegacyFallback
isLegacyPackage(pkgName) {
  const pkgInfo = this.getPackageInfo(pkgName);
  if (!pkgInfo) return false;
  
  // Check last update date
  const lastUpdated = new Date(pkgInfo.lastUpdated);
  const yearsOld = (Date.now() - lastUpdated) / (365 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000);
  if (yearsOld > 2) return true;
  
  // Check deprecated flag
  if (pkgInfo.deprecated) return true;
  
  return false;
}

Dependency emulation:
With mip legacy fix, MIP attempts to automatically resolve conflicts in legacy packages by creating dependency emulations that allow the package to work in a modern environment.


        
            🔍 Practical recommendations
            
        
        
What to do with legacy packages?

Check alternatives - often there are modern replacements:

request → axios or node-fetch
moment → day.js or date-fns
lodash → using built-in methods


Try updating - mip update <package> may solve the problem.

Use mip legacy fix - if automatic fix works, verify the application still functions correctly.

Plan migration - if the package is unmaintained, plan a transition to an alternative.


Important: Legacy packages are not always bad. If a package is stable and has no vulnerabilities, it can be kept. But regular auditing helps avoid future problems.

180.title: mip link - local package linking for development
180.text:
mip link


  
  

What is this?
mip link is a tool for local development of interconnected packages. Instead of constantly publishing changes to the registry and updating versions, you can create a global link to a local package and use it in other projects as a regular dependency. This significantly speeds up the development of libraries, plugins, and monorepos.

Syntax
mip link [package-name]

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




package-name
string
❌
Name of the package to link. If not specified - creates a link from the current package.



How it works
mip link (no arguments)

Checks for a config file (mip.yml or package.json) in the current directory.
Creates the global folder ~/.mip-links/.
Creates a symbolic link from ~/.mip-links/<package-name> to the current directory.
Outputs confirmation of successful link creation.

mip link <package-name>

Checks for the existence of a global link in ~/.mip-links/.
If the link is not found - displays a list of available links.
If the link is found:

Creates the node_modules/ folder (if it doesn’t exist).
Creates a symbolic link from node_modules/<package-name> to the target package.
Adds information about the linked package to the manifest (.mip/manifest.json).
Adds information about the linked package to the config (mip.yml).



Examples

Create a global link from the current packagecd ~/dev/my-lib
mip link

Example output:
✅ Created global link: my-lib -> /home/user/dev/my-lib

Now the package my-lib is globally available as my-lib.
== tab “Use a linked package in another project”
cd ~/dev/my-app
mip link my-lib

Example output:
✅ Added to manifest: my-lib@1.0.0
✅ Added to config: my-lib
✅ Linked my-lib@1.0.0 -> /home/user/dev/my-lib

Now my-app can use my-lib as a regular dependency.
:::
== tab “View available global links”
mip link unknown-package

Example output:
❌ No global link found for "unknown-package"

📦 Available links:
  • my-lib -> /home/user/dev/my-lib
  • shared-utils -> /home/user/dev/shared-utils

:::
== tab “Attempt to create a link without a config”
cd ~/dev/empty-folder
mip link

Example output:
❌ No config file found (mip.yml, mip.json, or package.json)
💡 Run "mip init" first

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ No config file found
Cause: No config file (mip.yml, mip.json, or package.json) in the current folder.
Solution:

Create mip.yml: mip init
Specify the package name in mip.yml.
Retry: mip link




  
  
  

❌ No global link found
Cause: You are trying to use a package that has not been globally linked.
Solution:

Navigate to the package folder you want to link.
Create a global link: mip link
Return to your project and use: mip link <package-name>




  
  
  
  

❌ Linked package is invalid
Cause: The target package is corrupted or deleted.
Solution:

Check that the target folder exists and contains a config.
If the package was moved - recreate the link: mip link in the package folder.




  
  
  

💡 Tip
mip link is ideal for:

Library development - test changes in a real project without publishing.
Monorepos - link packages together.
Plugins - develop plugins locally and use them in your project.

After changes in the package: changes are applied instantly (thanks to symbolic links). No need to reinstall the package!

Related commands

mip install - install packages from the registry.
mip uninstall - remove linked packages.
mip list - view installed packages (including linked ones).


        
            🧠 How does symbolic linking work?
            
        
        
MIP uses two levels of symbolic links:

Global link (~/.mip-links/<package-name> → <path-to-package>)

Created once for each package.
Allows referencing the package from any project.


Local link (node_modules/<package-name> → ~/.mip-links/<package-name>)

Created in each project that uses the linked package.
Points to the global link (which in turn points to the actual folder).



Advantages:

Changes in the package are visible instantly (no need to reinstall).
No need to publish intermediate versions to the registry.
Easy debugging.

Limitations:

Symbolic links do not work on all file systems (e.g., Windows may require administrator privileges).
When moving a package, links become invalid (need to be recreated).

190.title: mip list - list installed packages
190.text:
mip list


  
  
  
  
  
  

What is this?
mip list is a command for viewing all packages installed in the current project. It shows packages from the .mip/manifest.json manifest, their versions, and paths in the global cache. This is convenient for quickly checking dependency status.

Syntax
mip list

How it works

Check mip.yml - if the file is missing, the command outputs an error message.

Read manifest - all installed packages are extracted from .mip/manifest.json.

Determine source - for each package, the following is shown:

🌍 - package from the global cache ~/.mip/store/
🔗 - package linked via mip link
📁 - local package (for backward compatibility)


Display versions and paths - for each package, the version and path in the global cache are shown.

Total count - total number of packages.


Examples

View installed packagesmip list

Example output:
📦 Installed packages:

  🌍 express@4.18.2
     /home/user/.mip/store/express/4.18.2
  🌍 lodash@4.17.21
     /home/user/.mip/store/lodash/4.17.21
  🌍 jest@29.7.0
     /home/user/.mip/store/jest/29.7.0

📊 Total: 3 package(s)

== tab “Empty project (no packages)”
mip list

Example output:
📦 Installed packages:

  (no packages installed)

📊 Total: 0 package(s)

:::
== tab “Linked packages”
mip list

Example output:
📦 Installed packages:

  🔗 my-lib@1.0.0
     /home/user/dev/my-lib
  🌍 lodash@4.17.21
     /home/user/.mip/store/lodash/4.17.21

📊 Total: 2 package(s)

:::
Icon meanings



Icon
Meaning




🌍
Package from global cache (~/.mip/store/)


🔗
Locally linked package (via mip link)


📁
Local package (backward compatibility)



Common errors


  
  
  

❌ No mip.yml found
Cause: You are not in the root of a MIP project.
Solution:

Navigate to the project folder: cd /path/to/project
Make sure mip.yml exists there.
If not - create it: mip init




  
  
  

💡 Tip
mip list is the first command for diagnostics:

Check that all packages are installed.
Ensure paths in the manifest are correct.
Review package versions before updating.

Use mip list before mip outdated to understand what you have installed.

Related commands

mip install - install packages.
mip outdated - check for outdated packages.
mip doctor - project diagnostics.

198.title: mip repo - interactive GitHub repository browser
198.text:
mip repo


  
  

What is this?
mip repo is an interactive file manager for GitHub repositories. You can browse repository contents, navigate folders, read READMEs in full-screen mode, and download files locally - all without cloning the repository. The command uses the GitHub API and supports authentication via GITHUB_TOKEN to bypass rate limits.

Syntax
mip repo <owner/repo> [--branch <branch>] [--path <download-path>]

Arguments



Name
Type
Required
Description




owner/repo
string
✅
Repository owner and name (e.g., kiwinatra/mip)



Options



Flag
Description




--branch
Branch to browse (default: main)


--path
Path to save downloaded files (default: download/)



How it works

Connect to GitHub API - the command uses the public GitHub API to fetch repository contents.

Authentication - if the GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable is set, it is used to increase API rate limits.

Interactive mode - after entering the repository, an interactive shell opens with the following commands:

ls - show contents of the current folder.
cd <folder> - navigate to a folder.
cd .. - go up one level.
readme or cat - show README in full-screen mode.
get <filename> - download a file to the local folder.
help or ? - show help.
exit - exit the browser.



Examples

Browse mip repositorymip repo kiwinatra/mip

Example output:
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  mip repo: kiwinatra/mip/                                        ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
LICENSE
README.md
lib\
package.json
tests\

Commands: ls | cd <dir> | readme | get <file> | exit
mip repo>

== tab “Navigate folders”
mip repo kiwinatra/mip
mip repo> cd lib
mip repo> ls
mip repo> cd ../tests

Example output:
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  mip repo: kiwinatra/mip/tests/                                  ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
commands.test.js
install.test.js
utils.test.js

:::
== tab “Read README”
mip repo kiwinatra/mip
mip repo> readme

Opens full-screen README viewer:

j / ↓ - scroll down.
k / ↑ - scroll up.
PgDn / PgUp - page scroll.
q - exit read mode.
:::
== tab “Download a file”

mip repo kiwinatra/mip
mip repo> get package.json

Example output:
✅ Saved: package.json -> ./download/package.json

:::
== tab “Use with token”
export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_xxxxx
mip repo kiwinatra/mip

:::
Common errors


  
  
  

❌ Repository not found
Cause: Invalid owner or repository name.
Solution:

Check the spelling: owner/repo.
Make sure the repository is public (or you have access).




  
  
  

❌ API rate limit exceeded
Cause: GitHub API rate limit exceeded (60 requests/hour for unauthenticated users).
Solution:

Set GITHUB_TOKEN to increase the limit to 5000 requests/hour.
Wait an hour for the limit to reset.




  
  
  

❌ File not found
Cause: The specified file does not exist in the current folder.
Solution:

Use ls to view available files.
Make sure you are in the correct folder.




  
  
  

💡 Tip
mip repo is a powerful tool for:

Quickly browsing repository contents without cloning.
Reading documentation in repositories with large READMEs.
Selectively downloading files (saves time and bandwidth).

For private repositories, set GITHUB_TOKEN with read permissions.

Related commands

mip install - install packages (can be used with GitHub repositories).
mip info - package information from the registry.


        
            🧠 How does interactive mode work?
            
        
        
After entering the repository, a REPL shell (Read-Eval-Print Loop) opens where you can execute commands:

ls - queries the GitHub API for the current folder contents.
cd <folder> - updates the path and re-queries contents.
readme - searches for README files in the current folder (first looks for README.md, then other variants) and opens them in full-screen mode.
get <file> - downloads a file to the folder specified in --path.

Full-screen README mode:

Uses the terminal’s alternate screen.
Supports keyboard navigation.
Automatically adapts to window size.

README auto-detection:
The command looks for files in the following order:

README.md, README.MD, README.rst, README.txt.
Any file starting with readme..
Any file with .md extension.



        
            🔧 Using the GitHub API
            
        
        
mip repo uses the GitHub REST API to fetch repository contents:
Endpoint:
GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}

Authentication:
export GITHUB_TOKEN=your_personal_access_token

Rate limits:

Unauthenticated: 60 requests/hour.
Authenticated: 5000 requests/hour.

Response format:
[
  {
    "name": "README.md",
    "path": "README.md",
    "type": "file",
    "content": "base64...",
    "encoding": "base64"
  },
  {
    "n
